worried.heading: You're not alone in worrying
worried.intro: Noticing something's wrong is the first — and hardest — step. Here's what to look for, and how to help.

physical.title: Physical signs
items: Sudden weight loss or gain
Red or glazed eyes, small or large pupils
Deteriorating personal hygiene
Unexplained marks or bruising on arms
Tremors, slurred speech, impaired coordination
Unusual smells on breath or clothes

behavioural.title: Behavioural signs
items: Withdrawal from family, friends, or activities they used to enjoy
Sudden change in friends or social circle
Unexplained mood swings — irritable, secretive, or unusually energetic
Loss of interest in studies or work
Lying or making excuses about whereabouts
Money missing; asking for money with no clear reason
Staying out very late or disappearing for days

social.title: Social signs
items: Dropping grades or missing school/work
Getting into fights or legal trouble
Associating with people who use drugs
Giving up hobbies, sports, or creative interests

talkSteps: Choose a calm, private moment — not in the middle of a fight.
Use 'I' statements: 'I've noticed…' rather than 'You're always…'
Listen more than you speak. Don't lecture.
Express concern, not blame: your goal is connection, not confrontation.
Expect denial at first — that's normal. Keep the door open.
If they open up, ask what kind of support they want.
worried.helpText: You don't need to handle this alone. The Vimukthi helpline ({{vimukthi}}) offers free, confidential guidance for families too — not just for the person struggling.
worried.signsTapHint: Tap signs you've noticed — a pattern matters more than any single one
worried.chatMenuQuestion: What would you like help with?
worried.chatCategoryQuestion: Which type of signs have you noticed?
worried.chatSignsPrompt: Tap any signs you've noticed below. A pattern matters more than any single one.
worried.chatSignsSummary: {{count}} sign(s) noted. One sign alone doesn't confirm anything — but a pattern is worth paying attention to. What would you like next?

getHelp.heading: Help is here. You took the hardest step.
getHelp.intro: All Vimukthi centres are free, government-run, and confidential. You don't need a referral to call.
getHelp.locationNote: Your location is used only in your browser to sort centres by distance. It is never sent anywhere.
getHelp.locationDenied: Location access was not granted. Browse the list below.
getHelp.routeNeedLocation: Allow location to show the route on the map.
getHelp.counsellingNote: Not ready to visit a centre? Call the free Vimukthi counselling line first:
getHelp.usesHelplineNote: Until this centre's own number is verified, this call connects to the Vimukthi central line ({{vimukthi}}).
getHelp.directionsNotice: Tapping “Get directions” opens an external map (OpenFreeMap / OSRM or Google Maps) and shares your location with that service.

rights.heading: Treatment can protect you legally
rights.body: Choosing voluntary de-addiction treatment may grant immunity from prosecution for personal-use offences under Section 64A of the NDPS Act.

report.heading: Report safely — your identity is protected
report.intro: You can report suspected drug dealing or trafficking anonymously through official channels. This app does not receive, store, or forward any report. Your call or message goes directly to the authorities.

manas_call.title: MANAS National Helpline — {{manas}}
manas_call.desc: Free, 24/7. Report to the Narcotics Control Bureau (NCB) directly. You may remain anonymous.

manas_web.title: NCB Web Portal — {{ncbWeb}}
manas_web.desc: File an anonymous online report with the NCB. No phone needed.

manas_email.title: NCB Email — {{ncbEmail}}
manas_email.desc: Email a tip. Do not include your name if you wish to stay anonymous.

umang.title: UMANG App — MANAS Service
umang.desc: Open the UMANG government services app and search 'MANAS' to file a report.

wa_kerala.title: Kerala Police WhatsApp — {{keralaWa}}
wa_kerala.desc: Send details via WhatsApp to Kerala Police Anti-Narcotic Cell. You may remain anonymous.

antinarcotics_cell.title: Anti-Narcotic Special Cell
antinarcotics_cell.desc: Direct lines to Kerala Police Anti-Narcotic Special Cell.

emergency.title: Emergency — {{emergency}}
emergency.desc: If there is immediate danger to life, call {{emergency}}.

safetyTips: You do not have to give your name.
If you feel unsafe, leave first — then report.
report.privacy: This app does not see or store your report. Tapping a button opens your phone's call or WhatsApp app directly.

dataError.message: Could not load contact data. Check your connection and try again.

notFound.heading: Page not found
notFound.body: This page does not exist. Use the menu above or go back to the home page.

experiences.heading: Stories of hope
experiences.intro: Real experiences from people who found a way forward. Yours could inspire someone too.
experiences.privacyNote: Stories you write are saved only on this device — nothing is sent anywhere. Curated stories below are shared to inspire everyone.
experiences.shareIntro: What helped you? What do you wish someone had told you? Share it here — in your own words.
experiences.identityLabel: How should we show your name?
experiences.anonymousHint: Your story will appear as Anonymous — no name shown.
experiences.namedHint: Your first name will appear above your story.
experiences.storyPlaceholder: What happened, what helped, what you'd tell someone in the same place…
experiences.tooShort: Please write at least a few sentences.

stories.name: Arun
stories.text: I thought I had to handle it alone. Calling Vimukthi was the hardest and best thing I did. Counselling was free, no one judged me, and my family didn't even need to know at first.
A friend noticed I was slipping before I did. They didn't lecture — just said they were worried. That one conversation made it possible for me to ask for help.

stories.name: Priya
stories.text: Recovery isn't a straight line. I had setbacks. But every time I reached out again, someone was still there. That mattered more than being perfect.

parents.heading: For parents and teachers
parents.intro: You know this person better than anyone. Trust your instincts — and know there is help available.
parents.warningNote: No single sign confirms drug use. A pattern of several signs, or a sudden change from baseline, is more meaningful.
parents.talkNote: The goal is to keep the line of communication open, not to get a confession.
doItems: Find a calm, private moment (not mid-argument)
Acknowledge their feelings, even if you disagree
Make clear you love them regardless
dontItems: Don't search their room without a serious reason
Don't involve police as a first response for a teen who's experimenting
Don't react with panic — it shuts down conversation
nextSteps: Call Vimukthi ({{vimukthi}}) — they advise families, not just users.
Speak to a school counsellor if it involves a student.
Consider family counselling — addiction affects the whole family.
Look for a local support group (Al-Anon / Nar-Anon) for yourself.
If you believe there is immediate danger, call {{police}}.
teacherItems: Talk to the school counsellor first — don't handle it alone.
Document what you've observed factually (dates, behaviours).
Approach the student privately with care, not accusation.
Coordinate with parents sensitively — protect the student's dignity.
Know your school's referral pathway for professional support.
parents.signsTapHint: Tap signs you've noticed — a pattern matters more than any single one

screening.heading: Anonymous self-check
screening.intro: This is a brief, private self-reflection — not a medical test. Your answers are never saved and vanish the moment you leave this page.
screening.disclaimer: This is not a medical diagnosis. It is for personal reflection only. For any health concern, contact a qualified professional or call {{vimukthi}}.
screening.startPrompt: When you're ready, tap Begin self-check below.
screening.seeResultPrompt: All done — tap to see your result
questions: In the past year, have you used a substance (alcohol, drugs) more often or in larger amounts than you planned?
Have you tried to cut down or stop using a substance but found it difficult?
Has using substances taken up a lot of your time, or made it harder to keep up with work, studies, or responsibilities?
Have you continued using even though it was causing problems in your relationships or at home?
Have you noticed you need more of a substance to feel the same effect as before?
Have you felt strong urges or cravings to use a substance?
Have you felt down, anxious, or unwell when you stopped using or tried to cut back?

low.label: Low concern
low.message: Based on your answers, you don't seem to be struggling with substance use right now. If things change, or if you have any questions, the Vimukthi helpline ({{vimukthi}}) is always available — free and confidential.

moderate.label: Worth reflecting on
moderate.message: Some of your answers suggest it could be worth having a conversation with someone. This isn't an alarm — it's just a signal worth noticing. The Vimukthi counselling line ({{vimukthi}}) is free, confidential, and available to anyone — you don't need to have a 'serious problem' to call.

high.label: Consider reaching out
high.message: Your answers suggest you might benefit from talking to a professional. Please know this doesn't mean anything is 'wrong' with you — dependency is a medical condition, not a personal failing. Help is free, close by, and confidential. Call {{vimukthi}} or visit a Vimukthi centre near you.

screening.alwaysMessage: Whatever your result — talking to someone is always a good idea. Call {{vimukthi}} — free, confidential, no judgment.

about.heading: About lahariundo
about.mission: lahariundo is a free, non-commercial civic resource for Kerala's Operation Toofan anti-drug initiative. It connects people to official help through simple, mobile-friendly tools — with no accounts, no tracking, and no personal data stored on any server.

features.heading: What you can do here
features.intro: Every feature is free, private, and works without signing in. Tap what fits your situation.

items.title: What do I do?
items.desc: Chat-style guide — answer a few questions and get clear next steps for your situation.

items.title: Worried about someone
items.desc: Spot warning signs, learn how to talk without confrontation, and find support.

items.title: Get Help
items.desc: Find free government de-addiction centres on a map or browse by district.

items.title: Report Hub
items.desc: Search every official reporting channel — police, NCB, WhatsApp, web, and local offices.

items.title: Self-check
items.desc: Private, chat-style reflection tool — runs entirely in your browser, nothing saved.

items.title: Stories of hope
items.desc: Read inspiring recovery stories and share yours — first name or anonymous, saved on your device only.

items.title: Your rights
items.desc: Plain-language Section 64A, informant confidentiality, and free legal aid contacts.

items.title: Effects on body
items.desc: Factual, non-graphic health information — tap a body part to explore.

items.title: FAQ
items.desc: Tap a question, get an honest answer — with helplines and links when you need them.

items.title: Pledge card
items.desc: Create a drug-free pledge image to share — nothing is ever stored.

items.title: Operation Toofan snapshot
items.desc: Live progress figures with date and official source, right on the home page.

items.title: Quick helpline button
items.desc: Floating one-tap access to key official numbers on every screen.

specs.heading: How it's built
specs.intro: Technical details for anyone who wants to know what runs under the hood.

items.label: Type
items.value: Progressive Web App (PWA) — installable on phone or desktop, no app store needed

items.label: Languages
items.value: Full English and Malayalam UI — switch anytime

items.label: Design
items.value: Mobile-first layout with chat-style flows on Guide, Worried, Self-check, and FAQ

items.label: Privacy model
items.value: No accounts, no backend server, no database, no analytics that identify you

items.label: Self-check
items.value: Runs 100% in your browser — answers are never sent or stored anywhere

items.label: Stories
items.value: Stories you write stay in your browser's local storage only — never uploaded

items.label: Offline
items.value: Core pages work without internet after your first visit (service worker cache)

items.label: Themes
items.value: Light and dark mode with smooth theme transition

items.label: Map
items.value: Centre map uses OpenFreeMap tiles; directions use OSRM or Google Maps only when you tap 'Get directions'

items.label: Helplines
items.value: Tapping call or WhatsApp opens your phone's app directly — lahariundo never sees the contact

items.label: Data sources
items.value: Centre and helpline listings from public Kerala Police and Vimukthi Mission sources

items.label: Fonts
items.value: Bundled locally — no request to Google Fonts

noData.heading: Our promise: zero data collection
items: No user accounts. No login. No registration.
Self-check runs entirely in your browser — nothing is remembered when you leave.
Stories you write are kept only on your device (browser storage) — never sent to a server.
Your location is used in-browser to sort nearby centres; it only leaves your device if you tap 'Get directions'.
Helpline buttons open your phone's dialler or WhatsApp directly — this app never sees or records the call.
No advertising. No tracking pixels. No identifying analytics.
No backend server. Static site with no database.

thirdParty.heading: Third-party requests — full transparency
thirdParty.intro: The app stores nothing about you and has no database. A few features do load resources from outside services — here is exactly when that happens:
items: Map tiles load from OpenFreeMap when you open the centre map — like any website, this reveals your IP address to them.
Driving routes and directions are requested from OSRM and Google Maps only when you tap 'Get directions'.
The footer news ticker fetches public news headlines through our own news endpoint, which stores nothing and does not log you; tapping a headline opens that news website.
Fonts are bundled with the app — no request is ever made to Google Fonts.
None of these set advertising cookies or send data that identifies you.

sources.heading: Sources & verification
sources.text: Centre and helpline information is drawn from publicly available Kerala Police and Vimukthi Mission (Kerala Excise Department) sources. All numbers and addresses should be verified against the official sites before use:

about.builtBy: Built as a civic student project. Not affiliated with or endorsed by Kerala Police or the Kerala Government.
about.verifyNote: Warning: All phone numbers in this app are placeholders marked VERIFY_*. They must be replaced with verified official numbers before this app is published. Wrong contact info in a help app is dangerous.
about.contribute: Found an error? Contact us at: techsupport@ahimsa.global

resources.heading: Report Hub
resources.intro: Every official channel for reporting, help, and rights — in one place.
resources.privacyNote: Anonymous · direct to authorities · nothing stored here
resources.districtPending: Contact numbers pending verification — check your district police website or call {{police}}.
resources.districtNote: Contact details for district offices must be verified locally — check your district police website or call {{police}} to be connected.

rights.heading: Know Your Rights
rights.intro: Understanding Section 64A of the NDPS Act — how choosing treatment can protect you legally.
rights.disclaimer: NOT LEGAL ADVICE — This page contains general information about a law. It is not a substitute for advice from a qualified lawyer. Laws can change. Always consult a lawyer or the Legal Services Authority ({{legalAid}}) before making any decision.
rights.disclaimerShort: Not legal advice — tap to read full disclaimer
rights.chatMenuQuestion: What would you like to understand?

s64a.heading: Section 64A — Voluntary De-addiction & Immunity
s64a.whatBody: Under Section 64A of the Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances (NDPS) Act, 1985, a person who is an addict and has been charged with an offence under Section 27 (consumption of a narcotic drug or psychotropic substance) — or with possession of a small quantity for personal use — may voluntarily agree to undergo treatment and rehabilitation at a government-maintained hospital or a government-recognised de-addiction centre. If they do, the court may choose not to proceed with prosecution for that offence.
whatPoints: Section 64A lets someone charged with personal-use offences (Section 27 or small-quantity possession) choose voluntary treatment instead.
Treatment must be at a government hospital or government-recognised de-addiction centre.
If treatment is completed, the court may choose not to proceed with prosecution for that offence.
conditions: The person must voluntarily agree to treatment — it cannot be forced.
Treatment must be at a government hospital or a government-recognised facility (not just any private clinic).
The person must successfully complete the treatment programme.
The immunity is at the court's discretion — the court 'may' grant it, not 'must'.
If the person fails to complete treatment or leaves the programme early, the immunity is withdrawn and the case proceeds normally.
limitations: Section 64A covers consumption offences (Section 27) and small-quantity personal possession only.
It does NOT apply to trafficking, supply, larger quantities, or commercial offences.
It does not automatically erase the case — it depends on the court's decision.
It applies to the specific offence at hand, not to prior or unrelated charges.

confidentiality.heading: Informant Confidentiality
confidentiality.body: If you report drug activity to police or NCB, your identity as an informant is protected. Authorities are not required to disclose who made a report. You may report anonymously via {{manas}}, WhatsApp {{keralaWa}}, or the NCB web portal at {{ncbWeb}}.

legalAid.heading: Free Legal Help is Available
legalAid.body: If you or someone you know is facing drug-related charges and cannot afford a lawyer, free legal aid is available through the Legal Services Authority. Call {{legalAid}} to be connected to your district Legal Services Authority (DLSA).

emergency.heading: Emergency
emergency.intro: If someone is in immediate danger — call first, act second.

overdose.heading: If someone may have overdosed
overdose.callFirst: CALL {{emergency}} or {{ambulance}} FIRST — then follow these steps while help is on the way.
steps: Do not leave the person alone.
If unconscious but breathing, place them in the recovery position (on their side, chin forward) to prevent choking.
If they are not breathing and you know CPR, begin CPR and stay on the line with the emergency operator.
Do not give food, water, or any other substance.
Tell the ambulance operator what substance was taken if you know — this helps paramedics treat them faster. You will not get in trouble for calling.
overdose.recoveryDesc: Kneel beside the person. Extend their arm nearest to you. Pull the far knee up. Roll them gently toward you so they rest on their side. Tilt their head back slightly to keep the airway open. Stay with them.

emergency.privacy: This app does not log or store your call. Tapping a number opens your phone's dialler directly.

offline.offline: You're offline — saved pages still work
offline.mapBody: You're offline right now, so the map can't load. Reconnect to view it — or switch to the card list and helplines below, which work offline.

guide.heading: What do I do?
guide.intro: Answer a couple of simple questions and we'll point you to the right next step. Nothing you tap here is saved or sent anywhere.
guide.disclaimer: General guidance only — not legal or medical advice.

start.question: What's happening? Choose what fits best.

options.quit: I want to quit or get help
options.report: I want to report drug activity

options.public: In a public place or at school
options.home: At home — someone in my family

using_danger.question: Are they in immediate physical danger right now — unconscious, struggling to breathe, or having a seizure?

options.adult: An adult — a friend or family member

quit.question: What would help most right now?

options.centre: Find a treatment centre near me

report.question: How would you prefer to report?

options.danger: Someone is in danger right now

found_public.title: Reporting drugs found in public
steps: Don't touch or move anything — leave it where it is.
Quietly note the location and step away to somewhere safe.
If it's on school grounds, tell a teacher or staff member you trust.
Report it through an official channel — you can stay anonymous.

found_home.title: You found something at home
steps: Take a breath first — reacting in anger usually shuts the conversation down.
Choose a calm, private moment to talk, and lead with concern, not blame.
You don't have to handle this alone — free family counselling is available.
For help on how to talk to them, see 'Worried about someone'.

using_minor.title: Helping someone under 18
steps: Stay calm and supportive — they need to feel safe, not judged.
Childline is a free, 24/7 helpline for anything involving a child.
Free, confidential family counselling can guide your next steps.
The parents & teachers guide has more on how to start the conversation.

using_adult.title: Helping an adult you care about
steps: You can't force change, but you can keep the door open.
Talk with concern, not confrontation — listen more than you speak.
The Vimukthi line offers free, confidential guidance for families too.
When they're ready, free de-addiction centres are nearby.

quit_talk.title: Talking to someone is a strong first step
steps: You don't need to have a 'serious problem' to call.
The Vimukthi counselling line is free, confidential, and judgment-free.
You can call just to ask questions — there's no commitment.

quit_centre.title: Finding a centre near you
steps: Government de-addiction treatment is free and confidential.
Use the locator to find Vimukthi centres near you.
The national de-addiction helpline can also refer you to a centre.

quit_legal.title: Where you stand legally
steps: Choosing voluntary treatment can protect you under Section 64A of the NDPS Act.
Free legal aid is available if you're facing charges and can't afford a lawyer.
Read the plain-language summary of your rights.

report_call.title: Reporting by phone
steps: Never confront peddlers yourself — report from a safe place.
You do not have to give your name.
Call the MANAS national helpline or the Kerala anti-narcotic cell.

report_online.title: Reporting by message or online
steps: You can report anonymously — leave your name out if you prefer.
Send a WhatsApp tip to the Kerala Police anti-narcotic cell, or
File a report on the NCB web portal or by email.

overdose.title: This may be an emergency — act now
steps: Call {{emergency}} or {{ambulance}} immediately — don't wait.
Do not leave the person alone.
If unconscious but breathing, place them on their side (recovery position).
You will not get in trouble for calling for help.

effects.heading: How drugs affect the body
effects.intro: General, factual health information. Choose a substance, then tap an organ on the figure — or pick one from the list — to see how it can be affected. No instructions, no judgment — just facts.
effects.pickPart: Tap an organ to learn more
effects.noPart: Tap a highlighted organ on the figure, or choose one from the list below.
effects.nonGraphicNote: This is general educational information — not a diagnosis or a complete medical account. Effects vary from person to person.
effects.ctaText: Worried about yourself or someone else? Free, confidential help is available.
effects.source: Source: National Institute on Drug Abuse (NIDA) and public health authorities.
effects.affectedLegend: Highlighted organs are the ones most affected by this substance.
effects.emergencyText: Slowed breathing in an overdose is a medical emergency.
effects.selectOrgan: Select an organ to read more

recovery.heading: What getting help looks like
recovery.intro: Recovery isn't a single leap — it's a series of supported steps. Here's the path most people follow. Government de-addiction treatment in Kerala is free and confidential.
recovery.tapHint: Tap each step to read more

steps.title: Reaching out
steps.body: It starts with one conversation — a call, a visit, or telling someone you trust. You don't need to have hit 'rock bottom' to ask for help.
steps.reassure: Asking for help is a sign of strength, not weakness.

steps.title: Assessment
steps.body: A professional listens and helps make sense of what's going on — your health, your situation, and what kind of support fits you. Nothing is forced.
steps.reassure: This is a conversation, not a judgment.

steps.title: Detox (if needed)
steps.body: If the body has become dependent, medically supervised detox helps you through withdrawal safely and as comfortably as possible.
steps.reassure: You won't go through this alone — trained staff are with you.

steps.title: Counselling & therapy
steps.body: Talking therapy helps you understand the 'why' and build new ways to cope. It can be one-to-one, in groups, or with family.
steps.reassure: Setbacks are part of the process, not the end of it.

steps.title: Rehabilitation
steps.body: Rebuilding daily routines, skills and confidence — sometimes at a residential centre, sometimes while living at home.
steps.reassure: Progress at your own pace is still progress.

steps.title: Aftercare & reintegration
steps.body: Ongoing support — follow-ups, support groups, and help returning to study, work and relationships.
steps.reassure: Recovery is a journey you keep walking, with support along the way.

recovery.ctaText: There's no wrong time to start. Find a centre, or just talk to someone first.

faq.heading: Questions people are afraid to ask
faq.intro: Honest, plain answers to the things people worry about most.
faq.chatPickQuestion: Pick a question below to see the answer.
faq.disclaimer: General information only — not legal or medical advice.

items.q: Will I be arrested if I seek help?
items.a: Choosing voluntary de-addiction treatment can actually protect you. Under Section 64A of the NDPS Act, someone charged with a personal-use offence may avoid prosecution by completing treatment at a government-recognised centre. This is general information, not legal advice — read the full summary on the Your Rights page.

Yes. You can report drug activity without giving your name, and an informant's identity is protected. You can call the MANAS helpline, send a WhatsApp tip, or file online — all without identifying yourself.
Yes. Vimukthi de-addiction and counselling services run by the government are free and confidential, and you don't need a referral to call the helpline.

There's no fixed timeline — it depends on the person and the substance. Recovery is a series of steps, not a single event, and setbacks are a normal part of it. Free, confidential help is available whenever you're ready.

items.q: How do I help a friend who's using?
items.a: Lead with concern, not confrontation, and listen more than you speak. You can't force someone to change, but you can keep the door open and point them to free, confidential help when they're ready.
Childline is a free, 24/7 helpline for anything involving a child, including drug use. Approach the young person gently and without judgment — see 'Worried about someone' for how to start.

items.q: Does this app store anything I do?
items.a: No. There's no account and no database, and nothing you type or tap is saved or sent anywhere. Everything runs in your browser and resets when you close the app.

toofan.heading: Operation Toofan — progress snapshot
toofan.placeholder: Placeholder figures — not live data. Replace before publishing.
toofan.noData: Progress figures are not available right now.

pledge.heading: Make your pledge
pledge.intro: Create a drug-free pledge badge or card to share. Your name is used only to draw the image — it is never saved or sent anywhere.
pledge.heroSubtitle: Create your official drug-free badge in seconds — share it on WhatsApp, Instagram, or your profile.
pledge.readyBody: Tap share — friends see your pledge and can scan the QR to join.
pledge.shareCaptionLabel: Suggested caption (copy & paste with your image)
messages:
I choose a healthy life.
I stand against drugs.

Today I pledge to live free from drugs
and inspire others to do the same.
I stand for a drug-free Kerala.
I support my friends in staying drug-free.
pledge.shareTipWa: WhatsApp status or group — use the circular badge format
pledge.shareTipIg: Instagram story — use the pledge card format
pledge.shareTipQr: QR code on each card links back to LahariUndo for verification
pledge.shareTipBadge: Profile photo frame — download the badge and crop to a circle
pledge.shareText: I took the drug-free pledge with LahariUndo.
pledge.shareFailed: Sharing isn't available here — your card was downloaded instead.
pledge.privacy: Your name never leaves your device. The card is drawn entirely in your browser and is never stored or sent.

news.openArticle: Opens the news website in a new tab
